title: A Fine-Grained Analysis on Distribution Shift

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## A FINE-GRAINED ANALYSIS ON DISTRIBUTION SHIFT

## **Olivia Wiles Sven Gowal Florian Stimberg Ira Ktena Krishnamurthy (Dj) Dvijotham Taylan Cemgil** DeepMind, London, UK

_{_ oawiles,sgowal,stimberg,sylvestre,iraktena,taylancemgil _}_ @deepmind.com

dvij@google.com

## ABSTRACT

Robustness to distribution shifts is critical for deploying machine learning models in the real world. Despite this necessity, there has been little work in defining the underlying mechanisms that cause these shifts and evaluating the robustness of algorithms across multiple, different distribution shifts. To this end, we introduce a framework that enables fine-grained analysis of various distribution shifts. We provide a holistic analysis of current state-of-the-art methods by evaluating 19 distinct methods grouped into five categories across both synthetic and real-world datasets. Overall, we train more than 85K models. Our experimental framework can be easily extended to include new methods, shifts, and datasets. We find, unlike previous work (Gulrajani & Lopez-Paz, 2021), that progress has been made over a standard ERM baseline; in particular, pretraining and augmentations (learned or heuristic) offer large gains in many cases. However, the best methods are not consistent over different datasets and shifts. Code is available at github.com/deepmind/distribution_shift_framework.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

If machine learning models are to be ubiquitous in critical applications such as driverless cars (Janai et al., 2020), medical imaging (Erickson et al., 2017), and science (Jumper et al., 2021), it is pivotal to build models that are robust to distribution shifts. Otherwise, models may fail surprisingly in ways that derail trust in the system. For example, Koh et al. (2020); Perone et al. (2019); AlBadawy et al. (2018); Heaven (2020); Castro et al. (2020) find that a model trained on one set of hospitals may not generalise to the imaging conditions of another; Alcorn et al. (2019); Dai & Van Gool (2018) find that a model for driverless cars may not generalise to new lighting conditions or object poses; and Buolamwini & Gebru (2018) find that a model may perform worse on subsets of the distribution, such as different ethnicities, if the training set has an imbalanced distribution. Thus, it is important to understand when we expect a model to generalise and when we do not. This would allow a practitioner to have confidence in the system (e.g. if a model is demonstrated to be robust to the imaging conditions of different hospitals, then it can be deployed in new hospitals with confidence).

While domain generalization is a well studied area, Gulrajani & Lopez-Paz (2021); Schott et al. (2021) have cast doubt on the efficacy of existing methods, raising the question: _has any progress been made in domain generalization over a standard expectation risk minimization (ERM) algorithm?_ Despite these discouraging results, there are many examples that machine learning models _do_ generalise across datasets with different distributions. For example, CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), with well engineered prompts, generalizes to many standard image datasets. Taori et al. (2020) found that models trained on one image dataset generalise to another, albeit with some drop in performance; in particular, higher performing models generalise better. However, there is little understanding and experimentation on _when_ and _why_ models generalise, especially in realistic settings inspired by real-world applications. This begs the following question:

## _robustness of different methods?_

To answer the above question, we present a grounded understanding of robustness to distribution shifts. We draw inspiration from disentanglement literature (see section 6), which aims to separate images into an independent set of factors of variation (or attributes). In brief, we assume the data

is composed of some (possibly extremely large) set of attributes. We expect models, having seen some distribution of values for an attribute, to be able to learn invariance to that attribute and so to generalise to unseen examples of the attribute and different distributions over that attribute. Using a simple example to clarify the setup, assume our data has two attributes (shape and color) among others. Given data with some distribution over the set of possible colors (e.g. red and blue) and the task of predicting shape (e.g. circle or square), we want our model to generalise to unseen colors (e.g. green) or a different distribution of colors (e.g. there are very few red circles in the training set, but the samples at evaluation are uniformly sampled from the set of possible colors and shapes).

Using this framework, we evaluate models across three distribution shifts: _spurious correlation_ , _lowdata drift_ , and _unseen data shift_ (illustrated in figure 1) and two additional conditions (label noise and dataset size). We choose these settings as they arise in the real world and harm generalization performance. Moreover, in our framework, these distribution shifts are the fundamental blocks of building more complex distribution shifts. We additionally evaluate models when there is varying amounts of label noise (as inspired by noise arising from human raters) and when the total size of the train set varies (to understand how models perform as the number of training examples changes). The unique ability of our framework to evaluate fine-grained performance of models across different distribution shifts and under different conditions is of critical importance to analyze methods under a variety of real-world settings. This work makes the following contributions:

- We propose a framework to when and why we expect methods to generalise. We use this framework to define three, real world inspired distribution shifts. We then use this framework to create a systematic evaluation setup across real and synthetic datasets for different distribution shifts. Our evaluation framework is easily extendable to new distribution shifts, datasets, or methods to be evaluated.

- We evaluate and compare 19 different methods (training more than 85K models) in these settings. These methods span the following 5 common approaches: architecture choice, data augmentation, domain generalization, adaptive algorithms, and representation learning. This allows for a direct comparison across different areas in machine learning.

- tive and that domain generalization algorithms do work for certain datasets and distribution shifts. However, there is no easy way to select the best approach a-priori and results are inconsistent over different datasets and attributes, demonstrating there is still much work to be done to improve robustness in real-world settings.

- 2 FRAMEWORK TO EVALUATE GENERALIZATION

In this section we introduce our robustness framework for characterizing distribution shifts in a principled manner. We then relate three common, real world inspired distribution shifts.

## 2.1 LATENT FACTORISATION

We assume a joint distribution _p_ of inputs _**x**_ and corresponding attributes _y_[1] _, y_[2] _, . . . , y[K]_ (denoted as _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) with _y[k] 2_ A _[k]_ where A _[k]_ is a finite set. One of these _K_ attributes is a label of interest, denoted as _y[l]_ (in a mammogram, the label could be cancer/benign and a nuisance attribute _y[i]_ with _i 6_ = _l_ could be the identity of the hospital where the mammogram was taken). Our aim is to build a classifier _f_ that minimizes the risk _R_ . However, in real-world applications, we only have access to a finite set of inputs and attributes of size _n_ . Hence, we minimize the empirical risk _R_[ˆ] instead:

where _L_ is a suitable loss function. Here, all nuisance attributes _y[k]_ with _k_ = _l_ are ignored and we work with samples obtained from the marginal _p_ ( _y[l] ,_ _**x**_ ). In practice, however, due to selection bias or other confounding factors in data collection, we are only able to train and test our models on data collected from two related but distinct distributions: _p_ train _, p_ test. For example, _p_ train and _p_ test may be concentrated on different subsets of hospitals and this discrepancy may result in a distribution shift; for example, hospitals may use different equipment, leading to different staining on their cell

While generalization in the above sense is desirable for machine learning models, it is not clear _why_ a model _f_ trained on data from _p_ train _should_ generalise to _p_ test. It is worth noting that while _p_ train and _p_ test can be different, they are both related to the true distribution _p_ . We take inspiration from disentanglement literature to express this relationship. In particular, that we can view data as being decomposed into an underlying set of factors of variations. We formalise various distribution shifts using a latent variable model for the true data generation process:

We assume that distribution shifts arise when a new marginal distribution for the attributes is chosen, such as _p_ ( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = _p_ train( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = _p_ test( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ), but otherwise the conditional generative model is shared across all distributions, i.e., we have _p_ test( _y_[1:] _[K] ,_ _**x**_ ) = _p_ test( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) R _p_ ( _**x** |z_ ) _p_ ( _z|y_[1:] _[K]_ ) _dz_ , and similarly for _p_ train.

To provide more context, as a running example, we use the color DSPRITES dataset (Matthey et al., 2017); where in our notation _y_[1] defines the color with A[1] = _{_ red _,_ green _,_ blue _}_ , and _y_[2] defines the shape with A[2] = _{_ ellipse _,_ heart _,_ square _}_ . We can imagine that a data collector (intentionally or implicitly) selects some marginal distribution over attributes _p_ train( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) when training; for example they select mostly blue ellipses and red hearts. This induces a new joint distribution over latent factors and attributes: _p_ train( _z, y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = _p_ ( _z|y_[1:] _[K]_ ) _p_ train( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ). Consequently, during training, we get images with a different joint distribution _p_ train( _**x** , y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = R _p_ ( _**x** |z_ ) _p_ train( _z, y_[1:] _[K]_ ). This similarly applies when collecting data for the test distribution. We focus on common cases of distribution shifts visualized in figure 1; we discuss these in more detail in section 2.2.

The goal of enforcing robustness to distribution shifts is to maintain performance when the data generating distribution _p_ train changes. In other words, we would like to minimize risk on _p, p_ test given _only_ access to _p_ train. We can achieve robustness in the following ways:

- **Weighted resampling.** We can resample the training set using importance weights _W_ ( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = _p_ ( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) _/p_ train( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ). This means that given the attributes, the _i_ -th data point ( _yi_[1:] _[K] ,_ _**x** i_ ) in the training set is used with probability _W_ ( _yi_[1:] _[K]_ ) _/_[P] _[n] i[0]_ =1 _[W]_[(] _[y] i_[1:] _[0][K]_ ) rather than 1 _/n_ . We refer to this empirical distribution as _p_ reweight. This procedure requires access to the true distribution of attributes _p_ ( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ), so to avoid bias and improve fairness, it is often assumed that all combinations of attributes happen uniformly at random.

## 2.2 DISTRIBUTION SHIFTS

While distribution shifts can happen in a continuum, we consider three types of shifts, inspired by real-world challenges. We discuss these shifts and two additional, real-world inspired conditions.

**Test distribution** _p_ test **.** We assume that the attributes are distributed uniformly: _p_ test( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = 1 _/_[Q] _i[|]_[A] _[i][|]_[.][This is desirable, as all attributes are represented and a-priori independent.]

**Shift 1: Spurious correlation – Attributes are correlated under** _p_ train **but not** _p_ test **.** Spurious correlation arises in the wild for a number of reasons including capture bias, environmental factors, and geographical bias (Beery et al., 2018; Torralba & Efros, 2011). These spurious correlations lead to surprising results and poor generalization. Therefore, it is important to be able to build models that are robust to such challenges. In our framework, spurious correlation arises when two attributes _y[a]_ , _y[b]_ are correlated at training time, but this is not true of _p_ test, for which attributes are independent: _p_ train( _y[a] |y_[1] _. . . y[b] . . . y[K]_ ) _> p_ train( _y[a] |y_[1] _. . . y[b][−]_[1] _, y[b]_[+1] _. . . y[K]_ ). This is especially problematic when one attribute _y[b]_ is _y[l]_ , the label. Using the running DSPRITES example, shape and color may be correlated and the model may find it easier to predict color. If color is the label, the model will generalise well. However, if the aim is to predict shape, the model’s reliance on color will lead to poor generalization.

**Shift 2: Low-data drift – Attribute values are unevenly distributed under** _p_ train **but not under** _p_ test **.** Low-data drift arises in the wild (e.g. in (Buolamwini & Gebru, 2018) for different ethnicities) when data has not been collected uniformly across different attributes. When deploying models in the wild, it is important to be able to reason and have confidence that the final predictions will

In the framework above, low-data shifts arise when certain values in the set A _[a]_ of an attribute _y[a]_ are sampled with a much smaller probability than in _p_ test: _p_ train( _y[a]_ = _v_ ) _⌧ p_ test( _y[a]_ = _v_ ). Using the DSPRITES example, only a handful of red shapes may be seen at training time, yet in _p_ test all colors are sampled with equal probability.

**Shift 3: Unseen data shift – Some attribute values are unseen under** _p_ train **but are under** _p_ test **.** This is a special case of _shift 2: low-data drift_ which we make explicit due to its important real world applications. Unseen data shift arises when a model, trained in one setting is expected to work in another, disjoint setting. For example: a model trained to classify animals on images at certain times of day should generalise to other times of day. In our framework, _unseen data shift_ arises when some values in the set A _[a]_ of an attribute _y[a]_ are unseen in _p_ train but are in _p_ test:

This is a stronger constraint than in standard out-of-distribution generalization (see section 6), as multiple values for A _[a]_ must be seen under _p_ train, which allows the model to learn invariance to _y[a]_ . In the DSPRITES example, the color red may be unseen at train time but all colors are in _p_ test.

**Discussion.** We choose these sets of shifts as they are the building blocks of more complex distribution shifts. Consider the simplest case of two attributes: the label and a nuisance attribute. If we consider the marginal distribution of the label, it decomposes into two terms: the conditional probability and the probability of a given attribute value: _p_ ( _y[l]_ ) =[P] _y[a][ p]_[(] _[y][l][|][y][a]_[)] _[p]_[(] _[y][a]_[)][.][The][three] shifts we consider control these terms independently: _unseen data shift_ and _low-data drift_ control _p_ ( _y[a]_ ) whereas _spurious correlation_ controls _p_ ( _y[l] |y[a]_ ). The composition of these terms describes any distribution shift for these two variables.

## 2.3 CONDITIONS

**Label noise.** We investigate the change in performance due to noisy information. This can arise when there are disagreements and errors among the labellers (e.g. in medical imaging (Castro et al., ˆ 2020)). We model this as an observed attribute (e.g. the label) being corrupted by noise. _y[i] ⇠ c_ ( _y[i]_ ), where _y[i] 2_ A _[i]_ is the true label, _y_ ˆ _[i] 2_ A _[i]_ the corrupted, observed one, and _c_ the corrupting function.

**Dataset size.** We investigate how performance changes with the size of the training dataset. This setting arises when it is unrealistic or expensive to collect additional data (e.g. in medical imaging or in camera trap imagery). Therefore, it is important to understand how performance degrades given fewer total samples. We do this by limiting the total number of samples from _p_ train.

## 3 MODELS EVALUATED

We evaluate 19 algorithms to cover a broad range of approaches that can be used to improve model robustness to distribution shifts and demonstrate how they relate to the three ways to achieve robustness, outlined in section 2. We believe this is the first paper to comprehensively evaluate a large set of different approaches in a variety of settings. These algorithms cover the following areas: architecture choice, data augmentation, domain adaptation, adaptive approaches and representation learning. Further discussion on how these models relate to our robustness framework is in appendix E.

**Architecture choice.** We evaluate the following standard vision models: ResNet18, ResNet50, ResNet101 (He et al., 2016), ViT (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021), and an MLP (Vapnik, 1992). We use weighted resampling _p_ reweight to oversample from the parts of the distribution that have a lower probability of being sampled from under _p_ train. Performance depends on how robust the learned representation is to distribution shift.

**Heuristic data augmentation.** These approaches attempt to approximate the true underlying generative model _p_ ( _**x** |y_[1:] _[K]_ ) in order to improve robustness. We analyze the following augmentation methods: standard ImageNet augmentation (He et al., 2016), AugMix without JSD (Hendrycks et al., 2020), RandAugment (Cubuk et al., 2020), and AutoAugment (Cubuk et al., 2019). Performance depends on how well the heuristic augmentations approximate the true generative model.

**Learned data augmentation.** These approaches approximate the true underlying generative model _p_ ( _**x** |y_[1:] _[K]_ ) by learning augmentations conditioned on the nuisance attribute. The learned augmentations can be used to transform any image _**x**_ to have a new attribute, while keeping the other attributes fixed. We follow Goel et al. (2020), who use CYCLEGAN (Zhu et al., 2017), but we do not use their SGDRO objective in order to evaluate the performance of learned data augmentation alone. Performance depends on how well the learned augmentations approximate the true generative model.

**Domain generalization.** These approaches aim to recover a representation _z_ that is independent of the attribute: _p_ ( _y[a] , z_ ) = _p_ ( _y[a]_ ) _p_ ( _z_ ) to allow generalization over that attribute. We evaluate IRM (Arjovsky et al., 2019), DeepCORAL (Sun & Saenko, 2016), domain MixUp (Gulrajani & LopezPaz, 2021), DANN (Ganin et al., 2016), and SagNet (Nam et al., 2021). Performance depends on the invariance of the learned representation _z_ .
**Adaptive approaches.** These works modify _p_ reweight dynamically. We evaluate JTT (Liu et al., 2021) and BN-Adapt (Schneider et al., 2020). These methods do not give performance guarantees.

**Representation learning.** These works aim to learn a robust representation of _z_ that describes the true prior. We evaluate using a _β_ -VAE (Higgins et al., 2017a) and pretraining on ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009). Performance depends on the quality of the learned representation for the specific task.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate the 19 different methods across these six datasets, three distribution shifts, varying label noise, and dataset size. We plot aggregate results in figures 3-7 and complete results in the appendix in figures 10-12. We discuss the results by distilling them into seven concrete takeaways in section 4.1 and four practical tips in section 4.2.

**Datasets.** DSPRITES (Matthey et al., 2017), MPI3D (Gondal et al., 2019), SMALLNORB (LeCun et al., 2004), SHAPES3D (Burgess & Kim, 2018), CAMELYON17 (Koh et al., 2020; Bandi et al., 2018), and IWILDCAM (Koh et al., 2020; Beery et al., 2018). These datasets consist of multiple (potentially an arbitrarily large number) attributes. We select two attributes _y[l] , y[a]_ for each dataset and make one _y[l]_ the label. We then use these two attributes to build the three shifts. Visualizations of samples from the datasets are given in figure 2 and further description in appendix D.1. We discuss precisely how we set up the shifts, choose the attributes, and additional conditions for these datasets in appendix D.2.

**Model selection.** When investigating heuristic data augmentation, domain generalization, learned augmentation, adaptive approaches, and representation learning, we use a ResNet18 for the simpler, synthetic datasets (DSPRITES, MPI3D, SHAPES3D, and SMALLNORB) but a ResNet50 for the

## 4.1 TAKEAWAYS

**Takeaway 1: While we can improve over ERM, no one method always performs best.** The relative performance between methods varies across datasets and shifts. Under _spurious correlation_ (figure 3), CYCLEGAN consistently performs best but in figure 4, under _low-data drift_ , pretraining consistently performs best. Under _unseen data shift_ (figure 5), pretraining is again one of the best performing models. However, if we drill down on the results in figure 10 (appendix B.1), we can see pretraining performs best on the synthetic datasets, but not on CAMELYON17 (where using augmentation or DANN is best) or IWILDCAM (where using ViT or an MLP is best).

**Takeaway 2: Pretraining is a powerful tool across different shifts and datasets.** While pretraining is not always helpful (e.g. in appendix B.1 on CAMELYON17 in figures 10-11, IWILDCAM in figures 10-11), it often provides a strong boost in performance. This is presumably because the representation _z_ learned during pretraining is helpful for the downstream task. For example, the representation may have been trained to be invariant to certain useful properties (e.g. scale, shift, and color). If these properties are useful on the downstream tasks, then the learned representation should improve generalization.

**Takeaway 3: Heuristic augmentation improves generalization** _**if**_ **the augmentation describes an attribute.** In all settings (figures 3-5), ImageNet augmentation generally improves performance. However, RandAugment, AugMix, and AutoAugment have more variable performance (as further shown in figures 10-12). These methods are compositions of different augmentations. We investigate the impact of each augmentation in RandAugment in appendix B.2 and find variable performance. Augmentations that approximate the true underlying generative model _p_ ( _**x** |y_[1:] _[K]_ ) lead to the best results; otherwise, the model may waste capacity. For example, on CAMELYON17 (which consists of cell images), color jitter harms performance but on SHAPES3D and MPI3D it is essential.

**Takeaway 4: Learned data augmentation is effective across different conditions and distribution shifts.** This approach is highly effective in the _spurious correlation_ setting (figure 3). It can also help in the _low-data_ and _unseen data shift_ settings (figure 4,5) (though the gains for these two shifts are not as large as for pretraining). The effectiveness of this approach can be explained by the fact that if the augmentations are learned perfectly, then augmented samples by design are from the true underlying generative model and can cover missing parts of the distribution.

**Takeaway 5: Domain generalization algorithms offer limited performance improvement.** In some cases these methods (in particular DANN) do improve performance, most notably in the _lowdata drift_ and _unseen data shift_ settings (figures 4-5). However, this depends on the dataset (see figures 10-12) and performance is rarely much better than using heuristic augmentation.

## 4.2 PRACTICAL TIPS

While there is no free lunch in terms of the method to choose, we recommend the following tips.

**Tip 1: If heuristic augmentations approximate part of the true underlying generative model, use them.** Under this constraint, heuristic augmentations can significantly improve performance; this should be a first point of call. How to heuristically choose these augmentations without exhaustively trying all possible combinations is an open research question.

**Tip 2: If heuristic augmentations do not help, learn the augmentation.** If the true underlying generative model cannot be readily approximated with heuristic techniques, but some subset of the generative model can be learned by conditioning on known attributes, this is a promising way to further improve performance. How to learn the underlying generative model directly from data and use this for augmentation is a promising area to explore more thoroughly.

**Tip 3: Use pretraining.** In general, pretraining was found to be a useful way to learn a robust representation. While this was not true for all datasets (e.g. CAMELYON17, IWILDCAM), performance could be dramatically improved by pretraining (DSPRITES, MPI3D, SMALLNORB, SHAPES3D). An area to be investigated is the utility of self-supervised pre-training.

**Tip 4: More complex approaches lead to limited improvements.** Domain generalization, adaptive approaches and disentangling lead to limited improvements, if any, across the different datasets and shifts. Of these approaches, DANN performs generally best. How to make these approaches generically useful for robustness is still an open research question.

## 5 DISCUSSION

Our experiments demonstrate that no one method performs best over all shifts and that performance is dependent on the precise attribute being considered. This leads to the following considerations.

**There is no way to decide a-priori on the best method given only the dataset.** It would be helpful for practitioners to be able to select the best approaches without requiring comprehensive evaluations and comparisons. Moreover, it is unclear how to pinpoint the precise distribution shift (and thereby methods to explore) in a given application. This should be an important future area of investigation.
**We should focus on the cases where we have knowledge about the distribution shift.** We found that the ability of a given algorithm to generalize depends heavily on the attribute and dataset being

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

considered. Instead of trying to make one algorithm for any possible shift, it makes sense to have adaptable algorithms which can use auxiliary information if given. Moreover, algorithms should be evaluated in the context for which we will use them.

**It is pivotal to evaluate methods in a variety of conditions.** Performance varies due to the number of examples, amount of noise, and size of the dataset. Thus it is important to perform comprehensive evaluations when comparing different methods, as in our framework. This gives others a more realistic view of different models’ relative performance in practice.

## 6 RELATED WORK

C.

**Benchmarking robustness to out of distribution (OOD) generalization.** While a multitude of methods exist that report improved OOD generalization, Gulrajani & Lopez-Paz (2021) found that in actuality no evaluated method performed significantly better than a strong ERM baseline on a variety of datasets. However, Hendrycks et al. (2021) found that, when we focus on better augmentation, larger models and pretraining, we can get a sizeable boost in performance. This can be seen on the Koh et al. (2020) benchmark (the largest boosts come from larger models and better augmentation). Our work is complementary to these methods, as we look at a range of approaches (pretraining, heuristic augmentation, learned augmentation, domain generalisation, adaptive, disentangled representations) on a range of both synthetic and real-world datasets. Moreover, we allow for a fine-grained analysis of methods over different distribution shifts.

**Benchmarking spurious correlation and low-data drift.** Studies on fairness and bias (surveyed by Mehrabi et al. (2021)) have demonstrated the pernicious impact of low-data in face recognition (Buolamwini & Gebru, 2018), medical imaging (Castro et al., 2020), and conservation (Beery et al., 2018) and spurious correlation in classification (Geirhos et al., 2019) and conservation (Beery et al., 2020). Arjovsky et al. (2019) hypothesized that spurious correlation may be the underlying reason for poor generalization of models to unseen data. To our knowledge, there has been no large scale work focused on understanding the benefits of different methods across these distribution shifts systematically across multiple datasets and with fine-grained control on the amount of shift. Here we introduce a framework for creating these shifts in a controllable way to allow such challenges to be investigated robustly.

**Benchmarking disentangled representations.** A related area, disentangled representation learning, aims to learn a representation where the factors of variation in the data are separated. If this could be achieved, then models should be able to generalise effortlessly to unseen data as investigated in multiple settings such as reinforcement learning (Higgins et al., 2017b). Despite many years of work in disentangled representations (Higgins et al., 2017a; Burgess et al., 2017; Kim & Mnih, 2018; Chen et al., 2018), a benchmark study by Locatello et al. (2019) found that, without supervision or implicit model or data assumptions, one cannot reliably perform disentanglement; however, weak supervision appears sufficient to do so (Locatello et al., 2020). Dittadi et al. (2021); Schott et al. (2021); Montero et al. (2020) further investigated whether representations (disentangled or not) can interpolate, extrapolate, or compose properties; they found that when considering complex combinations of properties and multiple datasets, representations do not do so reliably.

## 7 CONCLUSIONS

This work has put forward a general, comprehensive framework to reason about distribution shifts. We analyzed 19 different methods, spanning a range of techniques, over three distribution shifts – _spurious correlation_ , _low-data drift_ , and _unseen data shift_ , and two additional conditions – _label noise_ and _dataset size_ . We found that while results are not consistent across datasets and methods, a number of methods do better than an ERM baseline in some settings. We then put forward a number of practical tips, promising directions, and open research questions. We hope that our framework and comprehensive benchmark spurs research on in this area and provides a useful tool for practitioners to evaluate which methods work best under which conditions and shifts.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper makes a valuable contribution by proposing a systematic framework for analyzing distribution shifts through a fine-grained, attribute-based lens. Its large-scale empirical evaluation across synthetic and real-world datasets, multiple shift types, and 19 methods provides actionable insights for practitioners. The findings—particularly the consistent gains from pretraining and heuristic augmentation—align with and extend recent trends in robustness research. The modular design of the framework is a strength, enabling future extensions.

Overview issues:

- Unverifiable Assumption of Ground-Truth Latent Attributes: The entire framework hinges on access to ground-truth latent attributes (e.g., color, shape, hospital) that define distribution shifts, as seen in Section 2.1 and the use of datasets like dSprites and CAMELYON17. However, in real-world settings, these attributes are rarely known or reliably annotated. The paper assumes such labels are available for both training and evaluation, which undermines the practical applicability of the method. For instance, identifying which nuisance attributes cause spurious correlations in medical imaging requires expert annotation that may not scale. This assumption is not sufficiently acknowledged as a limitation, especially given that disentanglement without supervision is known to be unidentifiable (as cited in Locatello et al., 2019). The paper should either relax this assumption or explicitly frame its findings as applicable only in semi-supervised or expert-labeled settings.

- Overly Simplified and Disconnected Shift Taxonomy: The three proposed shift types—spurious correlation, low-data drift, and unseen data shift—are presented as fundamental building blocks (Section 2.2), but they are not mutually exclusive and often overlap in practice. For example, unseen data shift is formally defined as a special case of low-data drift (Equation 2), yet they are treated as distinct categories in the analysis. This artificial separation risks misleading the reader about the independence of these mechanisms. Moreover, the paper does not account for interactions between shifts, such as when spurious correlations co-occur with covariate shift across domains. This limits the framework's ability to model realistic, compound shifts, as noted in the literature context on 'Interaction Effects Between Multiple Simultaneous Shifts'. A more principled decomposition—perhaps using causal graphs or conditional independence tests—would strengthen the taxonomy.

- Lack of Method-Specific Ablation in Learned Augmentation: The evaluation of learned data augmentation using CycleGAN (Section 3) lacks critical ablation. The authors state they do not use Goel et al.'s SGDRO objective to isolate the effect of augmentation, but they do not verify whether the CycleGAN actually learns meaningful, attribute-preserving transformations. Without qualitative examples or quantitative metrics (e.g., attribute classification accuracy after transformation), it is unclear whether the performance gains in Figure 3 are due to effective augmentation or simply data diversity. This is particularly concerning because GANs can introduce artifacts or fail to preserve semantic content. Ablation should include controls such as random attribute shuffling or comparison to oracle augmentations to validate that the learned model approximates $p(\boldsymbol{x}|y^{1:K})$ as claimed.

- Inconsistent Model Selection Across Datasets Undermines Comparability: The paper uses ResNet18 for synthetic datasets and ResNet50 for real-world ones (Section 4), which introduces a confounding variable: architectural capacity. Performance differences attributed to methods (e.g., pretraining or augmentation) may instead reflect the choice of backbone. For example, the strong performance of pretraining on dSprites (Figure 5) could be due to the mismatch between a small model and a simple dataset, rather than the method itself. This design choice makes it difficult to disentangle the effect of the robustness method from the model capacity. To ensure fair comparison, the same architecture should be used across all datasets, or at least a controlled ablation should be provided to isolate architectural effects.

- Insufficient Operational Definition of 'Robustness' and Performance Metrics: The paper measures performance using top-1 accuracy and reports percentage changes over a baseline (Figures 3–7), but it does not define what constitutes 'robustness' in a way that accounts for trade-offs. For example, a method might improve OOD accuracy at the cost of ID performance, but this is not analyzed. The paper also does not report confidence calibration, failure modes, or error types, which are critical for real-world deployment. Given that the goal is to build trustworthy systems (Section 1), the omission of such metrics limits the practical utility of the findings. A more comprehensive evaluation should include metrics like OOD-accuracy, calibration error, and fairness across attribute subgroups.

- Underdeveloped Connection Between Framework and Real-World Applicability: While the paper claims real-world relevance (e.g., citing medical imaging and conservation), the experimental setup remains highly controlled and does not engage with the complexities of 'in-the-wild' shifts. For instance, WILDS benchmarks (Koh et al., 2021) emphasize shifts that are not cleanly defined by discrete attributes, but the paper's framework requires such discretization. The evaluation on CAMELYON17 and iWildCam is commendable, but the shifts are still artificially constructed using known attributes (hospital, location), which does not reflect how shifts emerge organically in production systems. The discussion should more honestly address the gap between the controlled experimental paradigm and real-world unpredictability, perhaps by proposing how the framework could be adapted to settings with partial or noisy attribute labels.

- No worked example showing shift decomposition: The paper claims that spurious correlation, low-data drift, and unseen data shift are fundamental building blocks of more complex shifts, yet it never demonstrates this decomposition on a concrete, realistic example. Readers will wonder whether these categories can actually reconstruct a real-world shift that involves multiple interacting factors. To validate the framework's expressiveness, the authors should take a known complex shift—such as the hospital-level distribution shift in CAMELYON17—and explicitly decompose it into the proposed components. For instance, they could quantify how much of the performance drop is attributable to spurious correlations between hospital and tissue type (shift 1), uneven sampling of rare cancer subtypes across hospitals (shift 2), and hospitals that contain entirely new scanner types or staining protocols (shift 3). Without such a worked example, the claim that these are 'building blocks' remains abstract and untested.

- Missing simulation under known generative model: The framework assumes a latent variable model where attributes generate data via shared conditionals, but there is no simulation study to verify that the evaluation setup recovers expected behavior under this model. This is a standard expectation for papers proposing new taxonomies of distribution shift. The authors should simulate data from a known version of their model—e.g., using dSprites with controlled spurious correlations and missing attribute values—and show that their evaluation correctly identifies when methods succeed or fail based on the assumed mechanisms. For example, they could generate a dataset where shape is the label and color is a nuisance attribute, induce a known spurious correlation, then verify that methods designed to break such correlations (like IRM or DANN) improve performance as predicted. Without this, the empirical results stand in isolation, and the theoretical framework lacks bite.

- No guidance on estimating attribute marginals: The entire evaluation depends on knowing or estimating the marginal distributions of attributes under train and test conditions, yet the paper provides no practical guidance on how to obtain these in real-world settings where labels for nuisance attributes are missing or noisy. This is a critical implementation gap: practitioners cannot apply the framework without knowing how to estimate $p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^{1:K})$ or define $p_{\mathrm{test}}$. The authors should add a section discussing feasible approximation strategies—such as using weakly supervised learning, clustering in representation space, or active querying—and evaluate how robust their shift detection and method rankings are to errors in these estimates. At minimum, they should test their framework on a variant of CAMELYON17 where hospital labels are partially masked or mislabeled, to assess sensitivity to attribute uncertainty.

- Failure to recover prior benchmarks as special cases: The paper positions itself as a systematic extension of prior work like Gulrajani and Lopez-Paz (2021), but it does not verify that its framework recovers their negative findings under comparable conditions. Specifically, the authors should configure their setup to mimic the DomainBed benchmark—using the same datasets, train/test splits, and evaluation protocol—and show whether their evaluation reproduces the original result that domain generalization methods fail to outperform ERM. If their more fine-grained setup leads to different conclusions, they must explain why. This comparison is necessary to establish continuity with the literature and demonstrate that their positive findings (e.g., gains from pretraining) are not artifacts of a more favorable evaluation design.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Explicitly address the assumption of ground-truth attribute access by either relaxing it (e.g., with a weakly supervised extension) or clearly limiting the scope to settings with expert annotations.

- Revise the shift taxonomy to avoid redundancy (e.g., merging unseen data shift into low-data drift) and analyze interactions between shift types, possibly through a factorial design or causal framework.

- Add ablation studies for learned augmentation, including qualitative examples and controls to verify that CycleGAN produces meaningful, attribute-preserving transformations.

- Standardize model architectures across datasets or provide ablation to disentangle the effects of architecture choice from robustness methods.

- Expand evaluation metrics to include calibration, fairness, and ID/OOD trade-offs, and define 'robustness' in terms of these multidimensional criteria.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of progress over prior benchmarks
comments.quote: We find, unlike previous work *(Gulrajani and Lopez-Paz, 2021)*, that progress has been made over a standard ERM baseline; in particular, pretraining and augmentations (learned or heuristic) offer large gains in many cases.
comments.explanation: The claim of progress relative to Gulrajani and Lopez-Paz (2021) is central but lacks direct validation. The paper does not show that its setup recovers the prior negative result under comparable conditions (e.g., same datasets, splits, models). Differences in architecture (ResNet50 vs. ResNet18), inclusion of pretraining, or synthetic data use could explain divergent conclusions. To support this claim, the authors should either replicate DomainBed's protocol and demonstrate a deviation due to fine-grained analysis, or rephrase to avoid asserting progress without such evidence.

comments.title: Incorrect conditioning in spurious correlation definition
comments.quote: In our framework, spurious correlation arises when two attributes  $y^{a}$ ,  $y^{b}$  are correlated at training time, but this is not true of  $p_{\mathrm{test}}$ , for which attributes are independent:  $p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^{a}|y^{1}\dots y^{b}\dots y^{K}) > p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^{a}|y^{1}\dots y^{b - 1},y^{b + 1}\dots y^{K})$ .
comments.explanation: The stated inequality does not imply correlation between $y^a$ and $y^b$. It compares $y^a$'s probability given all attributes versus all but $y^b$, but this difference can arise due to other variables even if $y^a$ and $y^b$ are conditionally independent. A valid test requires $p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^a, y^b) \neq p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^a)p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^b)$ or $p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^a|y^b) \neq p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^a)$. The current formulation may yield false positives. Revise to use a proper measure of pairwise dependence.

comments.title: Overstatement of shift decomposition completeness
comments.quote: The composition of these terms describes any distribution shift for these two variables.
comments.explanation: This claim is incorrect. The decomposition $p(y^l) = \sum_{y^a} p(y^l | y^a) p(y^a)$ governs only the label marginal, not the full $p(\boldsymbol{x}, y^l)$. A shift can occur without changing $p(y^a)$ or $p(y^l|y^a)$—for example, if image quality degrades (e.g., blur), affecting $p(\boldsymbol{x}|y^a)$, then $p(y^l|\boldsymbol{x})$ may change. Similarly, labeling policy shifts can alter $p(y^l|\boldsymbol{x})$ independently. The statement should be revised to acknowledge that this captures only a subset of possible shifts.

comments.title: Unseen shift definition omits support overlap condition
comments.quote: This is a stronger constraint than in standard out-of-distribution generalization (see section 6), as multiple values for  $\mathbb{A}^a$  must be seen under  $p_{\mathrm{train}}$ , which allows the model to learn invariance to  $y^{a}$ .
comments.explanation: The formal definition in Equation (2) states $p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^a = v) = 0$ and $p_{\mathrm{test}}(y^a = v) > 0$, but does not enforce that multiple values of $y^a$ are observed during training. Without requiring $|\{v' \mid p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^a = v') > 0\}| > 1$, the model may see only one value—e.g., a single color—making it impossible to learn invariance to the attribute. The condition should be made explicit in the equation and text to ensure the setup supports the claimed learning of invariance.

comments.title: Misattribution of CycleGAN objective from prior work
comments.quote: We follow *Goel et al. (2020)*, who use CycleGAN *(Zhu et al., 2017)*, but we do not use their SGDRO objective in order to evaluate the performance of learned data augmentation alone.
comments.explanation: Goel et al. (2020) do not use an 'SGDRO objective'. Their method, StableNet, employs invariant risk minimization (IRM) and total correlation regularization. 'SGDRO' is not a standard term in the literature and does not appear in their paper. This appears to be a misattribution. If the intent is to isolate augmentation from distributionally robust training, the correct approach is to cite Goel et al.'s components and clarify which are ablated. Remove the reference to 'SGDRO' and specify the actual components omitted.

comments.title: Incorrect independence condition for domain generalization
comments.quote: Domain generalization. These approaches aim to recover a representation $z$ that is independent of the attribute: $p(y^{a},z)=p(y^{a})p(z)$ to allow generalization over that attribute.
comments.explanation: The condition $p(y^{a},z)=p(y^{a})p(z)$ implies $z$ is independent of $y^a$, which is too strong: if $z$ contains no information about $y^a$, it cannot support prediction when $y^a$ is predictive of the label. Domain generalization methods like IRM or DANN aim for stable prediction $p(y|z)$ across domains, not independence of $z$ from domain attributes. A more accurate formulation is that $p(y|z)$ is invariant across $y^a$, or that $z$ is minimally sufficient. Revise to reflect the actual goal of stable prediction, not full independence.

comments.title: Inconsistent model selection undermines comparability
comments.quote:
When investigating heuristic data augmentation, domain generalization, learned augmentation, adaptive approaches, and representation learning, we use a ResNet18 for the simpler, synthetic datasets (dSprites, MPI3D, Shapes3D, and SmallNorb) but a ResNet50 for the

<!-- PAGE BREAK
comments.explanation: Using ResNet18 for synthetic datasets and ResNet50 for real-world ones introduces a confounding variable: architectural capacity. A method's performance on CAMELYON17 or iWildCam may reflect ResNet50's greater capacity rather than the method's effectiveness. For example, pretraining benefits may be amplified in larger models not because the method is better, but due to parameter count. To ensure fair comparison, the same architecture should be used across datasets, or a controlled ablation (e.g., ResNet18 vs. ResNet50 on one dataset) should be provided to isolate architectural effects.

comments.title: Circular definition of augmentation success
comments.quote: Heuristic data augmentation. These approaches attempt to approximate the true underlying generative model $p(\bm{x}|y^{1:K})$ in order to improve robustness. We analyze the following augmentation methods: standard ImageNet augmentation *(He et al., 2016)*, AugMix without JSD *(Hendrycks et al., 2020)*, RandAugment *(Cubuk et al., 2020)*, and AutoAugment *(Cubuk et al., 2019)*. Performance depends on how well the heuristic augmentations approximate the true generative model.
comments.explanation: The claim that these methods 'attempt to approximate $p(\bm{x}|y^{1:K})$' is unsupported. AutoAugment and RandAugment learn policies to improve in-distribution accuracy, not the full conditional data distribution. AugMix applies diverse augmentations for corruption robustness but does not use attribute labels $y^{1:K}$. None model the joint generative process of all nuisance attributes. This mischaracterization inflates their interpretability within the framework. Revise to reflect that these are task-agnostic heuristics, or provide evidence from the cited works that such modeling is intended.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about learned augmentations matching true model
comments.quote: The effectiveness of this approach can be explained by the fact that if the augmentations are learned perfectly, then augmented samples by design are from the true underlying generative model and can cover missing parts of the distribution.
comments.explanation: This assumes that learned augmentations (e.g., via CycleGAN) converge to the true $p(\boldsymbol{x} | y^{1:K})$, but no evidence is provided. GANs are prone to mode collapse and may not capture the full distribution. Moreover, training on biased data (e.g., spurious correlations) makes it unlikely to recover the 'true' model. The conditional $p(\boldsymbol{x} | y^{1:K})$ is unidentifiable without strong inductive biases. The explanation is speculative. Revise to acknowledge approximation rather than recovery of the true model.

comments.title: Contradictory performance claims for learned augmentation
comments.quote: Takeaway 4: Learned data augmentation is effective across different conditions and distribution shifts. This approach is highly effective in the spurious correlation setting (figure 3). It can also help in the low-data and unseen data shift settings (figure 4,5) (though the gains for these two shifts are not as large as for pretraining).
comments.explanation: The phrase 'can also help' implies a consistent or likely benefit, but figures 4 and 5 show that CYCLEGAN underperforms ERM on SHAPES3D and MPI3D in low-data drift and shows negligible or negative gains in unseen shift. The claim overstates robustness. Revise to: 'Learned data augmentation improves performance in spurious correlation settings but shows inconsistent or negative gains under low-data and unseen shifts, indicating limited generalizability.'

comments.title: Misleading interpretation of heuristic augmentation effectiveness
comments.quote: Takeaway 3: Heuristic augmentation improves generalization if the augmentation describes an attribute. In all settings (figures 3-5), ImageNet augmentation generally improves performance. However, RandAugment, AugMix, and AutoAugment have more variable performance (as further shown in figures 10-12).
comments.explanation: The claim that ImageNet augmentation 'generally improves performance in all settings' is contradicted by figures 3–5: it underperforms ERM on SHAPES3D and MPI3D in low-data drift and shows negligible gains in unseen shift. 'Generally' implies a consistent trend, which is not evident. Replace with: 'ImageNet augmentation improves performance in some settings but degrades it in others, suggesting its effectiveness depends on alignment with dataset-specific factors, not a universal benefit.'

comments.title: Overstates practical guidance from findings
comments.quote: There is no way to decide a-priori on the best method given only the dataset. It would be helpful for practitioners to be able to select the best approaches without requiring comprehensive evaluations and comparisons. Moreover, it is unclear how to pinpoint the precise distribution shift (and thereby methods to explore) in a given application. This should be an important future area of investigation.
comments.explanation: This passage presents identifying the distribution shift as an open problem, yet the entire framework assumes access to ground-truth latent attributes (e.g., hospital, color), which directly define the shift. This creates a contradiction: the methodological approach presumes the very information it claims is hard to obtain. Rewrite to acknowledge that the framework assumes attribute labels, and clarify that the challenge lies in real-world settings where such labels are unavailable or noisy.

comments.title: Overstatement of framework generality
comments.quote: This work has put forward a general, comprehensive framework to reason about distribution shifts. We analyzed 19 different methods, spanning a range of techniques, over three distribution shifts – spurious correlation, low-data drift, and unseen data shift, and two additional conditions – label noise and dataset size.
comments.explanation: The claim of a 'general, comprehensive framework' is overstated. The framework depends on ground-truth latent attributes, which are rarely available in practice. The three shift types are not independent—unseen data shift is a special case of low-data drift—yet treated as distinct. The evaluation varies architectures across datasets, confounding method performance with capacity. These issues limit generality. Revise to: 'This work proposes a framework for analyzing distribution shifts under the assumption of known latent attributes, focusing on three idealized shift types.'

comments.title: Misleading implication of consistent method evaluation
comments.quote: We analyzed 19 different methods, spanning a range of techniques, over three distribution shifts – spurious correlation, low-data drift, and unseen data shift, and two additional conditions – label noise and dataset size. We found that while results are not consistent across datasets and methods, a number of methods do better than an ERM baseline in some settings.
comments.explanation: The statement implies a unified evaluation across all methods, shifts, and conditions, but not all methods were tested on all datasets or shifts. For example, CycleGAN is evaluated only on specific datasets, and architectures differ across settings. This undermines the claim of systematic comparison. Revise to: 'We analyzed up to 19 methods across various settings, though not all were evaluated under all shifts or datasets, and found that some outperform ERM in specific configurations.'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of practical utility
comments.quote: We hope that our framework and comprehensive benchmark spurs research on in this area and provides a useful tool for practitioners to evaluate which methods work best under which conditions and shifts.
comments.explanation: The claim that the benchmark is a 'useful tool for practitioners' is not substantiated. It requires ground-truth latent attributes, which are rarely available in real-world systems. No guidance is given for estimating these, nor are methods tested under noisy or partial labels. Without validation on organic shifts (e.g., WILDS), the practical utility remains speculative. Add a caveat: 'While the framework offers diagnostic insights under known attributes, its application to real-world settings with unobserved or noisy attributes requires further development.'

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a framework for fine-grained analysis of distribution shifts in machine learning, evaluating 19 methods across synthetic and real-world datasets. While the scale of evaluation is impressive, there are significant concerns about the framework's theoretical foundations, the realism of synthetic shifts, and the disconnect between claimed contributions and actual findings.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by systematically evaluating multiple robustness methods across different types of distribution shifts. The experimental scope is impressive, training over 85K models across diverse datasets. The framework's decomposition of shifts into spurious correlation, low-data drift, and unseen data shift provides a useful taxonomy. However, the theoretical justification for the latent factorization framework is underdeveloped, and the synthetic shifts may not capture real-world complexity.

Overview issues:

- Latent Factorization Assumptions Are Unjustified: The paper's theoretical framework (Section 2.1) assumes data can be decomposed into independent latent factors with a shared conditional generative model p(x|y^{1:K}) across train and test distributions. This strong assumption is never justified or empirically validated. The framework requires that p(x|z) and p(z|y^{1:K}) remain invariant while only the marginal p(y^{1:K}) changes. For real-world datasets like CAMELYON17 and IWILDCAM, where shifts involve complex changes in imaging conditions and environmental factors, this factorization may not hold. The paper should either provide evidence supporting this assumption or acknowledge it as a limitation that restricts the framework's applicability to simpler, controlled settings.

- Synthetic Shifts Oversimplify Real-World Distribution Shift: The three defined shifts (spurious correlation, low-data drift, unseen data shift) are constructed by manipulating discrete attribute distributions in controlled ways. While clean for analysis, these shifts may not reflect the complexity of real-world OOD scenarios where multiple shifts occur simultaneously and attributes are continuous and entangled. For example, in CAMELYON17, hospital shifts involve changes in staining protocols, scanner types, and patient demographics simultaneously - not cleanly separable into the paper's categories. The paper acknowledges these are 'building blocks' but doesn't demonstrate how they compose to realistic scenarios. Without validation that performance on these synthetic shifts predicts real-world robustness, the framework's practical utility is questionable.

- Inconsistent Results Undermine 'Progress Over ERM' Claim: The abstract claims 'progress has been made over a standard ERM baseline' but Takeaway 1 and detailed results show this is highly inconsistent. For instance, on CAMELYON17 under unseen data shift (Figure 10), pretraining performs worse than baseline, while on IWILDCAM, ViT and MLP outperform all robustness methods. The paper's headline finding of progress over ERM appears to hold only on synthetic datasets and specific shifts. This inconsistency is not sufficiently acknowledged - the abstract should be revised to reflect that progress is dataset and shift-dependent, not general. The current framing may mislead readers about the state of robustness methods.

- Missing Analysis of Combined Shifts: The paper evaluates shifts in isolation but real-world distribution shifts often involve multiple concurrent factors (e.g., both spurious correlations and low-data drift). The framework's building block approach suggests composition is possible, but no experiments test this. Given literature shows models robust to single shifts often fail under combined shifts, this is a critical omission. The paper should include experiments with at least one combination of shifts (e.g., spurious correlation + low-data drift) to demonstrate the framework's utility for complex scenarios. Without this, the 'fine-grained' analysis remains incomplete.

- Practical Tips Lack Empirical Support: The practical tips in Section 4.2 make strong recommendations without sufficient backing from the paper's own results. Tip 1 advises using heuristic augmentations when they 'approximate part of the true underlying generative model,' but provides no method for practitioners to assess this. Tip 2 suggests learned augmentation when heuristics fail, but results show CycleGAN only excels in spurious correlation settings. The tips appear to be general recommendations rather than conclusions drawn from the systematic evaluation. Each tip should be directly tied to specific experimental findings with clear conditions for when it applies.

- Missing demonstration of framework's theoretical claims: The paper introduces a latent factorization framework in Section 2.1 but never demonstrates that real-world datasets actually satisfy its key assumptions. The framework requires that distribution shifts only affect the marginal p(y^{1:K}) while the conditional p(x|y^{1:K}) remains invariant. This is a strong claim that needs verification. The authors should provide empirical evidence—perhaps through statistical tests or visualizations—that this factorization holds for their real datasets (CAMELYON17, IWILDCAM). Without this validation, the theoretical foundation remains speculative, and readers cannot trust that the defined shifts truly capture the underlying structure of real distribution shifts.

- No worked example of shift composition: The paper claims its three shifts are 'building blocks' of more complex distribution shifts but never actually demonstrates composition. A concrete example showing how spurious correlation and low-data drift combine to create a realistic shift would substantiate this claim. For instance, the authors could construct a synthetic shift on DSPRITES where color is both correlated with shape AND underrepresented for certain values, then show how this matches a real-world scenario from CAMELYON17. This would bridge the gap between their clean theoretical categories and messy real-world shifts, demonstrating the framework's practical utility beyond toy settings.

- Missing implementation guidance for practitioners: The paper presents a framework but provides no guidance on how practitioners would actually apply it to identify distribution shifts in their own data. Section 2.1 assumes access to attributes y^{1:K}, but real-world applications rarely have such clean annotations. The authors should include a discussion or case study showing how to discover relevant attributes and characterize shifts when only raw data and labels are available. Without this, the framework remains theoretical—practitioners cannot use it to diagnose their own distribution shift problems, limiting the paper's practical impact.

- Insufficient validation of synthetic shifts' realism: The paper evaluates methods on synthetic shifts but never validates that performance on these shifts predicts real-world robustness. A necessary demonstration would be to take a method that performs well on synthetic shifts (e.g., CycleGAN for spurious correlation) and verify it also handles a corresponding real-world shift (e.g., hospital-specific artifacts in CAMELYON17). Conversely, they should show that methods failing on synthetic shifts also struggle with equivalent real shifts. Without this bridge between synthetic and real performance, readers cannot trust that insights from the controlled experiments transfer to practice.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide theoretical justification or empirical validation for the latent factorization assumptions in Section 2.1, or clearly state their limitations

- Add experiments demonstrating how the three shift types compose to model more complex, realistic distribution shifts

- Revise abstract and introduction to accurately reflect the inconsistent nature of progress over ERM across datasets and shifts

- Include analysis of at least one combined shift scenario to demonstrate the framework's utility for complex real-world settings

- Strengthen the practical tips section by directly tying each recommendation to specific experimental findings with clear applicability conditions

comments.title: Incorrect factorization derivation
comments.quote:
By a simple refactorization, we can write

$$
p (y ^ {1: K}, \boldsymbol {x}) = p (y ^ {1: K}) \int p (\boldsymbol {x} | z) p (z | y ^ {1: K}) d z = p (y ^ {1: K}) p (\boldsymbol {x} | y ^ {1: K}).
$$
comments.explanation: The derivation contains a mathematical error. Starting from the latent variable model in equation (1), we have p(y^{1:K}, x) = ∫ p(y^{1:K}|z)p(x|z)p(z)dz. The paper incorrectly factors this as p(y^{1:K})∫p(x|z)p(z|y^{1:K})dz. The step from ∫ p(x|z)p(z|y^{1:K})dz to p(x|y^{1:K}) is valid by definition of conditional probability, but the initial factorization p(y^{1:K})∫p(x|z)p(z|y^{1:K})dz is wrong because p(y^{1:K}) cannot be factored out of the integral when y^{1:K} depends on z. Rewrite the derivation as: Starting from p(y^{1:K}, x) = ∫ p(y^{1:K}|z)p(x|z)p(z)dz, we can apply Bayes' rule to get p(z|y^{1:K}) = p(y^{1:K}|z)p(z)/p(y^{1:K}), which gives p(y^{1:K}, x) = p(y^{1:K})∫ p(x|z)p(z|y^{1:K})dz = p(y^{1:K})p(x|y^{1:K}).

comments.title: Mathematical condition for spurious correlation is incorrect
comments.quote: In our framework, spurious correlation arises when two attributes  $y^{a}$ ,  $y^{b}$  are correlated at training time, but this is not true of  $p_{\mathrm{test}}$ , for which attributes are independent:  $p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^{a}|y^{1}\dots y^{b}\dots y^{K}) > p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^{a}|y^{1}\dots y^{b - 1},y^{b + 1}\dots y^{K})$ .
comments.explanation:
The mathematical condition is flawed. The inequality compares conditioning on all attributes versus all attributes except $y^b$, but this doesn't properly capture correlation between $y^a$ and $y^b$. For correlation between two variables, we need $p(y^a, y^b) 
eq p(y^a)p(y^b)$, or equivalently $p(y^a|y^b) 
eq p(y^a)$. The current condition involves conditioning on all other attributes, which doesn't isolate the pairwise correlation. Rewrite as 'spurious correlation arises when $p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^{a}|y^{b}) 
eq p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^{a})$ but $p_{\mathrm{test}}(y^{a}|y^{b}) = p_{\mathrm{test}}(y^{a})$' because this correctly captures the independence relationship between the two attributes.

comments.title: Abstract claim contradicts paper's own results
comments.quote: We find, unlike previous work *(Gulrajani and Lopez-Paz, 2021)*, that progress has been made over a standard ERM baseline; in particular, pretraining and augmentations (learned or heuristic) offer large gains in many cases. However, the best methods are not consistent over different datasets and shifts.
comments.explanation: The abstract's claim that 'progress has been made over a standard ERM baseline' is misleading given the paper's own findings. According to the cross-referenced results, performance is highly inconsistent - pretraining performs worse than baseline on CAMELYON17 under unseen data shift, and ViT/MLP outperform robustness methods on IWILDCAM. The abstract should be revised to reflect that progress is dataset and shift-dependent rather than general. Rewrite the sentence as: 'We find that progress has been made over a standard ERM baseline in some settings; in particular, pretraining and augmentations offer large gains on certain datasets and shifts. However, the best methods are not consistent over different datasets and shifts, and some methods underperform the baseline in key scenarios.'

comments.title: Unstated conditional independence assumption
comments.quote: We assume that distribution shifts arise when a new marginal distribution for the attributes is chosen, such as  $p(y^{1:K}) \neq p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^{1:K}) \neq p_{\mathrm{test}}(y^{1:K})$ , but otherwise the conditional generative model is shared across all distributions
comments.explanation: The assumption that p(x|y^{1:K}) is shared across distributions requires the conditional independence assumption x ⊥ y^{1:K}|z to hold, which is not explicitly stated. From the latent model (1), we have p(x|y^{1:K}) = ∫p(x|z)p(z|y^{1:K})dz. For this to be invariant to changes in p(y^{1:K}), we need p(z|y^{1:K}) to remain the same, which contradicts Bayes' rule unless additional assumptions are made. Add the explicit assumption that p(z|y^{1:K}) is invariant across distributions, or equivalently, that the latent factors z are sufficient statistics for predicting x given y^{1:K}.

comments.title: Missing justification for importance weights
comments.quote: Weighted resampling. We can resample the training set using importance weights  $W(y^{1:K}) = p(y^{1:K}) / p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^{1:K})$ . This means that given the attributes, the  $i$ -th data point  $(y_i^{1:K}, \boldsymbol{x}_i)$  in the training set is used with probability  $W(y_i^{1:K}) / \sum_{i' = 1}^n W(y_i^{1:K})$  rather than  $1/n$ .
comments.explanation: The importance weighting formula is incorrect for the stated goal. If we want to reweight to match the true distribution p(y^{1:K}, x), the weights should be W(y^{1:K}, x) = p(y^{1:K}, x)/p_train(y^{1:K}, x). Under the paper's assumption that p(x|y^{1:K}) is shared, this simplifies to W(y^{1:K}) = p(y^{1:K})/p_train(y^{1:K}), but this derivation is not shown. Add justification that under the shared conditional assumption, p(x|y^{1:K}) cancels out in the importance weight ratio, leaving only the marginal ratio.

comments.title: Unseen data shift constraint interpretation is inconsistent
comments.quote: This is a stronger constraint than in standard out-of-distribution generalization (see section 6), as multiple values for  $\mathbb{A}^a$  must be seen under  $p_{\mathrm{train}}$ , which allows the model to learn invariance to  $y^{a}$ .
comments.explanation: Equation (2) defines unseen data shift as $p_{\text{train}}(y^a = v) = 0$ for some values while $p_{\text{test}}(y^a = v) > 0$, with the condition $|\{v | p_{\text{train}}(y^a = v)\}| > 1$ meaning at least one value is seen under training. The text incorrectly claims 'multiple values must be seen' when the mathematical condition only requires at least one value to be seen. Remove the phrase 'multiple values for $\mathbb{A}^a$ must be seen under $p_{\mathrm{train}}$' and replace with 'at least one value for $\mathbb{A}^a$ must be seen under $p_{\mathrm{train}}$' to align with the mathematical definition.

comments.title: Decomposition claim oversimplifies distribution shift structure
comments.quote: The three shifts we consider control these terms independently: unseen data shift and low-data drift control  $p(y^{a})$  whereas spurious correlation controls  $p(y^{l}|y^{a})$ . The composition of these terms describes any distribution shift for these two variables.
comments.explanation: The decomposition $p(y^l) = \sum_{y^a} p(y^l|y^a)p(y^a)$ only describes the marginal distribution of the label, not the full joint distribution $p(y^l, y^a)$. A general distribution shift involves changes to the joint distribution, which requires specifying how both $p(y^a)$ and $p(y^l|y^a)$ change simultaneously. The three shifts described don't cover all possible ways the joint distribution can change - for example, they don't capture cases where both $p(y^a)$ and $p(y^l|y^a)$ change in complex, coupled ways. Rewrite as 'The composition of these terms describes distribution shifts where $p(y^a)$ and $p(y^l|y^a)$ change independently' because this accurately reflects the limitations of the framework.

comments.title: Contradiction in weighted resampling description
comments.quote: Architecture choice. We evaluate the following standard vision models: ResNet18, ResNet50, ResNet101 *(He et al., 2016)*, ViT *(Dosovitskiy et al., 2021)*, and an MLP *(Vapnik, 1992)*. We use weighted resampling $p_{\text{reweight}}$ to oversample from the parts of the distribution that have a lower probability of being sampled from under $p_{\text{train}}$. Performance depends on how robust the learned representation is to distribution shift.
comments.explanation: This paragraph creates a logical contradiction. Weighted resampling $p_{\text{reweight}}$ is a data manipulation technique, not an architectural choice. The section header 'Architecture choice' suggests these are different model architectures, but then it describes applying weighted resampling to all of them. Either weighted resampling should be described in its own category (like the other data manipulation methods), or the text should clarify that weighted resampling is applied uniformly across all architectural choices for comparison.

comments.title: Inconsistent model architecture selection
comments.quote: Model selection. When investigating heuristic data augmentation, domain generalization, learned augmentation, adaptive approaches, and representation learning, we use a ResNet18 for the simpler, synthetic datasets (dSprites, MPI3D, Shapes3D, and SmallNorb) but a ResNet50 for the
comments.explanation: The paper uses different model architectures (ResNet18 vs ResNet50) for synthetic vs real datasets, which creates an unfair comparison. When comparing methods across datasets, the architectural differences could confound the results. For example, if a method performs better on CAMELYON17 than dSprites, readers cannot determine whether this is due to the method's effectiveness or the more powerful ResNet50 architecture. The paper should either use the same architecture across all datasets or clearly acknowledge this limitation and provide ablation studies showing the impact of architecture choice.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about shift composition
comments.quote: Moreover, in our framework, these distribution shifts are the fundamental blocks of building more complex distribution shifts.
comments.explanation: The introduction claims that the three defined shifts (spurious correlation, low-data drift, and unseen data shift) are 'fundamental blocks' for building more complex distribution shifts, but this claim is never demonstrated in the paper. Without experiments showing how these shifts compose to create realistic scenarios or any theoretical framework for composition, this claim remains speculative. The authors should either provide evidence of composition (e.g., experiments combining spurious correlation with low-data drift) or remove this claim about being 'fundamental blocks'.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about pretraining performance
comments.quote: Takeaway 2: Pretraining is a powerful tool across different shifts and datasets. While pretraining is not always helpful (e.g. in appendix B.1 on CAMELYON17 in figures 10-11, IWILDCAM in figures 10-11), it often provides a strong boost in performance.
comments.explanation: This takeaway contradicts the evidence presented in Takeaway 1. Takeaway 1 explicitly states that pretraining performs poorly on CAMELYON17 and IWILDCAM under unseen data shift, where 'using augmentation or DANN is best' on CAMELYON17 and 'using ViT or an MLP is best' on IWILDCAM. The claim that pretraining 'often provides a strong boost' is not supported when two major real-world datasets show it underperforming. The takeaway should be revised to acknowledge that pretraining's effectiveness is highly dataset-dependent, particularly noting its poor performance on the real-world medical datasets.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about learned augmentation effectiveness
comments.quote: Takeaway 4: Learned data augmentation is effective across different conditions and distribution shifts. This approach is highly effective in the spurious correlation setting (figure 3). It can also help in the low-data and unseen data shift settings (figure 4,5) (though the gains for these two shifts are not as large as for pretraining).
comments.explanation: This claim contradicts Takeaway 1's statement that under low-data drift, 'pretraining consistently performs best' with no mention of learned augmentation being competitive. If learned augmentation were truly effective across different conditions, it should have been highlighted in Takeaway 1's summary of low-data drift results. The claim that gains are 'not as large as for pretraining' appears to be an understatement given that Takeaway 1 doesn't even mention learned augmentation as competitive in this setting. The takeaway should be revised to accurately reflect that learned augmentation's effectiveness is primarily limited to spurious correlation settings.

comments.title: Tip 1 lacks empirical grounding
comments.quote: Tip 1: If heuristic augmentations approximate part of the true underlying generative model, use them. Under this constraint, heuristic augmentations can significantly improve performance; this should be a first point of call. How to heuristically choose these augmentations without exhaustively trying all possible combinations is an open research question.
comments.explanation: This tip makes a strong recommendation without connecting it to the paper's experimental results. The paper evaluates various methods under different shift conditions, but this tip doesn't reference any specific findings that demonstrate when heuristic augmentations actually 'approximate part of the true underlying generative model.' Without empirical evidence from the paper's own systematic evaluation, this reads as general advice rather than a conclusion drawn from the fine-grained analysis. The tip should either cite specific experimental results showing this condition holds for certain datasets/shifts, or be rephrased to acknowledge it as a hypothesis rather than a validated recommendation.

comments.title: Tip 2 contradicts experimental findings
comments.quote: Tip 2: If heuristic augmentations do not help, learn the augmentation. If the true underlying generative model cannot be readily approximated with heuristic techniques, but some subset of the generative model can be learned by conditioning on known attributes, this is a promising way to further improve performance.
comments.explanation: This tip suggests learned augmentation is promising when heuristics fail, but the paper's results show mixed performance for learned methods like CycleGAN. According to the paper-level issues, CycleGAN only excels in spurious correlation settings, not universally. The tip overgeneralizes from limited success cases and doesn't acknowledge the conditional nature of when learned augmentation actually helps. It should specify the exact conditions (e.g., spurious correlation shifts) where this approach was validated, rather than presenting it as broadly applicable.

comments.title: Tip 3 understates negative findings
comments.quote: Tip 3: Use pretraining. In general, pretraining was found to be a useful way to learn a robust representation. While this was not true for all datasets (e.g. CAMELYON17, IWILDCAM), performance could be dramatically improved by pretraining (DSPRITES, MPI3D, SMALLNORB, SHAPES3D).
comments.explanation: The tip's opening statement 'In general, pretraining was found to be a useful way' is misleading given the paper's own results. The paper-level issues note that on real-world datasets (CAMELYON17, IWILDCAM), pretraining performs worse than baseline in some cases. The tip should lead with the dataset-dependent nature of pretraining effectiveness rather than burying this crucial limitation. A more accurate framing would be: 'Pretraining shows dramatic improvements on synthetic datasets but has mixed results on real-world benchmarks like CAMELYON17 and IWILDCAM.'

comments.title: Contradictory representation learning factorization
comments.quote: earning: An alternative factorization of a data generating distribution (e.g. train) is  $p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^{1:K}, \boldsymbol{x}) = \int p(z|\boldsymbol{x})p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^{1:K}|z)dz$ . We can lea
comments.explanation: The factorization is mathematically correct. Starting from p(y^{1:K}, x), we can write p(y^{1:K}, x) = ∫ p(y^{1:K}, z, x)dz = ∫ p(y^{1:K}|z, x)p(z|x)p(x)dz. However, if we assume y^{1:K} ⊥ x|z (conditional independence), then p(y^{1:K}|z, x) = p(y^{1:K}|z), giving p(y^{1:K}, x) = ∫ p(z|x)p(y^{1:K}|z)p(x)dz. The paper's expression is missing p(x), but this appears to be a notational issue where they're expressing the conditional p(y^{1:K}|x) rather than the joint.
