The authors should either quote the actual conclusion (e.g., 'weak supervision can help but does not guarantee disentanglement') or remove the claim. Rewrite as: 'Locatello et al. (2020) found that weak supervision can improve disentanglement but does not guarantee it.'

comments.title: Unsupported claim about effortless generalization from disentanglement
comments.quote: If this could be achieved, then models should be able to generalise effortlessly to unseen data as investigated in multiple settings such as reinforcement learning *(Higgins et al., 2017b)*.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'effortlessly generalise' is an overstatement not supported by the cited reference. Higgins et al. (2017b) show that disentangled representations can improve sample efficiency in reinforcement learning, but they do not claim 'effortless' generalization to unseen data. The paper's own later discussion (citing Dittadi et al., Schott et al., Montero et al.) actually contradicts this optimism by showing that representations do not generalize reliably. This creates an internal inconsistency: the section first claims effortless generalization is possible, then immediately cites evidence that it is not. The authors should either remove the word 'effortlessly' or qualify the claim (e.g., 'may improve generalization in some settings').

comments.title: Citation of Dittadi et al. (2021) does not match described findings
comments.quote: *Dittadi et al. (2021); Schott et al. (2021); Montero et al. (2020)* further investigated whether representations (disentangled or not) can interpolate, extrapolate, or compose properties; they found that when considering complex combinations of properties and multiple datasets, representations do not do so reliably.
comments.explanation: The description conflates the findings of three papers. Dittadi et al. (2021) specifically study whether disentangled representations improve performance on downstream tasks (like classification) and find that they do not consistently outperform non-disentangled representations. The paper does not primarily study interpolation/extrapolation/composition of properties. The authors should either cite the correct finding from Dittadi et al. (2021) or remove the citation and rely on Schott et al. and Montero et al., which do study composition. Rewrite as: 'Schott et al. (2021) and Montero et al. (2020) found that representations do not reliably interpolate, extrapolate, or compose properties across complex combinations.'

comments.title: Overclaiming framework generality
comments.quote: This work has put forward a general, comprehensive framework to reason about distribution shifts.
comments.explanation: The paper's framework assumes a latent factorisation with discrete, independent attributes and a fixed conditional generative model. This is a strong assumption that is not empirically validated and may not hold for many real-world shifts. The claim of generality is overstated given these limitations. Rewrite as 'This work has put forward a framework to reason about distribution shifts under the assumption of latent factorisation, and we have analyzed its implications for three specific shift types.'

comments.title: Claim of comprehensive benchmark unsupported
comments.quote: We hope that our framework and comprehensive benchmark spurs research on in this area and provides a useful tool for practitioners to evaluate which methods work best under which conditions and shifts.
comments.explanation: The benchmark covers only 6 datasets (4 synthetic, 2 real-world) and 19 methods, with no statistical significance testing. The paper does not provide a tool for practitioners to evaluate methods on their own data, nor does it validate the building-block decomposition. The term 'comprehensive' is misleading. Remove 'comprehensive' and add a sentence acknowledging the limited scope: 'Our benchmark covers a specific set of synthetic and real-world datasets; further validation on diverse domains is needed.'

comments.title: Equation (2) set notation is ambiguous
comments.quote: p _ {\text {t r a i n}} \left(y ^ {a} = v\right) = 0 \quad p _ {\text {t e s t}} \left(y ^ {a} = v\right) > 0 \quad | \{v | p _ {\text {t r a i n}} \left(y ^ {a} = v\right) \} | > 1 \tag {2}
comments.explanation: The third condition in equation (2) uses the set builder notation $\{v \mid p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^a = v)\}$, which is ambiguous because $p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^a = v)$ is a probability value, not a Boolean condition. The intended meaning is that at least two distinct values of attribute $y^a$ have nonzero probability under $p_{\mathrm{train}}$. Rewrite the condition as $|\{v : p_{\mathrm{train}}(y^a = v) > 0\}| > 1$ to be mathematically precise.

comments.title: Incomplete derivation of factorization
comments.quote:
By a simple refactorization, we can write

$$
p (y ^ {1: K}, \boldsymbol {x}) = p (y ^ {1: K}) \int p (\boldsymbol {x} | z) p (z | y ^ {1: K}) d z = p (y ^ {1: K}) p (\boldsymbol {x} | y ^ {1: K}).
$$

Thus, the true distribution can be expressed as the product of the marginal distribution of the attributes with a conditional generative model.
comments.explanation: The derivation glosses over the definition of p(z|y^{1:K}). From the model in (1), the joint is p(z, y^{1:K}, x) = p(z) ∏_i p(y^i|z) p(x|z). Marginalizing over z gives p(y^{1:K}, x) = ∫ p(z) ∏_i p(y^i|z) p(x|z) dz. To rewrite this as p(y^{1:K}) ∫ p(x|z) p(z|y^{1:K}) dz, one must define p(z|y^{1:K}) = p(z) ∏_i p(y^i|z) / p(y^{1:K}), which is Bayes' rule. This step is mathematically valid under the model but is not explicitly stated. The derivation would be clearer if the authors wrote p(x|y^{1:K}) = ∫ p(x|z) p(z|y^{1:K}) dz and noted that this follows from conditional independence. Rewrite the quoted text as: 'By a simple refactorization using the conditional independence assumptions in (1), we can write p(y^{1:K}, x) = p(y^{1:K}) ∫ p(x|z) p(z|y^{1:K}) dz = p(y^{1:K}) p(x|y^{1:K}), where p(z|y^{1:K}) = p(z) ∏_i p(y^i|z) / p(y^{1:K}) by Bayes' rule.'
